sources_audited.note: The Census manual confirms the 512250 definition, included operating functions, revenue forms, and cross-referenced exclusions. It is definitional evidence and does not measure the numeric eligibility, retention, or future operator-required shares for which it is also cited.

The May 2024 OEWS table confirms the cited leading occupations and headcounts for the broader Sound Recording Industries population. It provides neither a complete wage-weighted task inventory nor a 512250-specific AI-exposure or implementation measure.

BLS confirms computer-based recording and editing, equipment operation, quality control, problem reporting, and recordkeeping duties for broadcast, sound, and video technicians. It does not document the commercial, rights, royalty, or administrative workflows that account for much of the stated exposure bridge.

BLS reports NAICS 512200 output of $31.4 billion in 2024 and $45.1 billion in 2034 in chained 2017 dollars, with a 3.7% compound annual growth rate. The record accurately labels this as a broader-industry ten-year proxy.

RIAA confirms 2025 US wholesale revenue of $11.5 billion, streaming at 82% of revenue, 106.5 million paid accounts, and continued vinyl growth. These aggregate nominal market facts do not directly measure LMM 512250 demand, benefit retention, or future operator necessity.

Gallup states that its fall-2024 survey found 22% of employer-business owners versus 9% of nonemployers planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The record accurately discloses that this is an all-industry intentions benchmark rather than completed 512250 control transfers.

caveats: The Census definition supports the industry boundary but not the estimated values for e, q, or o; those values remain openly labeled judgments.
The a bridge starts from only the largest occupations in broader NAICS 512200, without a complete occupation distribution, wage weights, or a reproducible task-to-exposure calculation.
The task evidence in S3 concerns technical recording occupations and does not directly support the commercial, rights, royalty, and administrative workflows emphasized in the a and rho rationales.
No cited source measures implemented AI labor savings in 512250, so rho and the resulting H factor remain model-conditioned despite plausible digital-workflow reasoning.

The d5 base converts a ten-year broader-industry BLS projection into a five-year 512250 estimate and applies an unsourced judgmental discount; the bridge and uncertainty range disclose this limitation.
RIAA's aggregate nominal market statistics support demand context but cannot isolate constant-price demand, retention, or operator necessity for screened LMM labels and distributors.
The s5 proxy uses broad ownership intentions rather than completed qualifying control transfers; the discount to 0.14 is plausible but not empirically identified for record labels or distributors.
summary: Mechanics pass and all six primitive-cited sources were reopened. The cited occupation mix, technician duties, broader-industry real-output projection, recorded-music market statistics, succession-intention benchmark, and official NAICS boundary are accurately represented. The record remains useful because it labels the decisive numeric judgments as PROXY or ESTIMATE, supplies broad ranges and explicit bridges, and keeps the action at VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS. The principal limitations are incomplete and broader-industry labor evidence, no observed AI implementation or benefit-retention data, aggregate rather than LMM demand evidence, and an all-industry intentions proxy for transfers. None is a material finding under the required test: an evidence-state downgrade would not change the already MODEL_CONDITIONED action, and the sources do not establish a correction that would change the LOW_PRIORITY base tier.
